coredata.dc:title: Highly efficient red-emitting carbon dots as a “turn-on” temperature probe in living cells
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Qin
Tang, Zhihua
Li, Lihua
Guo, Jinxiu
Jin, Lingxia
Lu, Jiufu
Huang, Pei
Zhang, Shengrui
Jiao, Long
coredata.dc:description: Nanothermometers, which can precisely detect the intracellular temperature changes, have great potential to solve questions concerning the cellular processes. Thus, the temperature sensors that provide fluorescent “turn-on” signals in the biological transparency window are of highly desirable. To meet these criteria, this work reported a new “turn-on” carbon dot (CD)-based fluorescent nanothermometry device for sensing temperature in living cells. The CDs that emit bright red fluorescence (R-CDs; λmax = 610 nm in water) were synthesized with o-phenylenediamine as carbon precursor via a facile solvothermal method. The R-CDs in water were almost nonfluorescent at 15 °C. As the temperature increased, the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs exhibited a gradual increase and the final enhancement factor was greater than 21-fold. The fluorescence intensity exhibited a linear response to temperature and a high-sensitive variation of ≈13.3 % °C−1 was detected within a broad temperature range of 28–60 °C. Moreover, the R-CD thermal sensors also exhibited high storage stability, excellent response reversibility and superior photo- and thermo-stability. Due to its good biocompatibility and “intelligent” response to external temperature, the nanothermometer could be applied for sensing temperature changes in biological media.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Temperature probe
High quantum yield
Intracellular temperature change
Bioimaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: In vitro intracellular thermal sensing with R-CDs
Insight into the temperature-dependent fluorescence of R-CDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge the financial support by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21775118, 22177066), Innovation Capability Support Program of Shaanxi (Nos. 2020KJXX-030, 2021KJXX-51), Scientific Research Program Funded by Education Department of Shaanxi Provincial Government (20JK0575), Co-construction Project of Hanzhong city and Shaanxi University of Technology (SXJ-2103, SXJ-2105) and Open Foundation of Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Natural Functional Molecule Chemistry of Ministry of Education (KLSNFM2020007).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Reaction mixtures prepared at different volumes of concentrated HCl under 365 nm UV light. From left to right, the volumes of concentrated HCl were 10, 20, 40, 60 and 80 μL, respectively. (B) Reaction mixtures prepared at different reaction time. From left to right, the reaction times were 3, 6, 9, 12 and 15 h, respectively.
TEM and HRTEM (inset) images of the R-CD solution at 25 °C.

Characterization of the functional groups and structure of R-CDs. XPS (A) and FT-IR (B) spectra of R-CDs. High-resolution XPS survey scan spectra of C 1s (C), N 1s (D), O 1s (E) and Cl 2p3 (F) of R-CDs.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectrum and excitation (λem = 610 nm) band of the R-CDs in aqueous solution; (B) Fluorescent emission spectra of R-CDs in ethanol solution under different excitation wavelengths.

(A) Fluorescent emission spectra of R-CDs in aqueous solution at a temperature range of 15–60 °C. Inset: The images of R-CDs at 20 °C and 60 °C under 365 nm UV light. (B) A linear relationship between the temperature and fluorescence intensity of R-CDs.

(A) Normalized transient intensity of R-CDs on the nanosecond time and microsecond time scale (inset); (B) Temperature dependence of the quantum yields of R-CDs, and red line is the linear regression using Eq. (1).

(A) Schematic diagram of the HeLa cells incubated in a culture medium containing R-CDs; (B) Schematic diagrams of R-CDs and (C) HeLa cells; (D) Cytotoxic effects of R-CDs against HeLa cells upon 24 h of incubation.
Confocal microscopic images of the Hela cells stained with R-CDs at 30 °C, 35 °C and 40 °C, respectively. Scale bar: 10 μm. (λex = 543 nm; λem = 555–655 nm).
Confocal fluorescence microscopic images of the Salmonella cells stained with R-CDs at 30 °C and 40 °C. Scale bar: 5 μm. (λex = 543 nm; λem = 555–655 nm).
A comparison of CD-based methods for detection of temperature.

row.entry: Not real-time monitoring intracellular temperature value

head.ce:title: Highly efficient red-emitting carbon dots as a “turn-on” temperature probe in living cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shaanxi Key Laboratory of Catalysis, School of Chemistry and Environment Science, Shaanxi University of Technology, Hanzhong, Shaanxi 723000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Environment Science

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science, Gansu Agricultural University, Lanzhou 730070, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Science, Gansu Agricultural University, Lanzhou 730070, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Xi’an Shiyou University, Xi’an 710065, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Xi’an Shiyou University, Xi’an 710065, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Schematic presentation of highly efficient red-emitting carbon dots as a “turn-on” nanothermometer for real-time monitoring intracellular temperature value. This nanoprobe has a high accuracy and sensitivity for detecting temperature changes.

Nanothermometers, which can precisely detect the intracellular temperature changes, have great potential to solve questions concerning the cellular processes. Thus, the temperature sensors that provide fluorescent “turn-on” signals in the biological transparency window are of highly desirable. To meet these criteria, this work reported a new “turn-on” carbon dot (CD)-based fluorescent nanothermometry device for sensing temperature in living cells. The CDs that emit bright red fluorescence (R-CDs; λmax = 610 nm in water) were synthesized with o-phenylenediamine as carbon precursor via a facile solvothermal method. The R-CDs in water were almost nonfluorescent at 15 °C. As the temperature increased, the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs exhibited a gradual increase and the final enhancement factor was greater than 21-fold. The fluorescence intensity exhibited a linear response to temperature and a high-sensitive variation of ≈13.3 % °C−1 was detected within a broad temperature range of 28–60 °C. Moreover, the R-CD thermal sensors also exhibited high storage stability, excellent response reversibility and superior photo- and thermo-stability. Due to its good biocompatibility and “intelligent” response to external temperature, the nanothermometer could be applied for sensing temperature changes in biological media.

ce:section.ce:para: Temperature is a fundamental parameter in biological systems, which affects many intracellular processes and chemical reactions [1]. Sensing of intracellular temperature is of great importance to study the effects of external factors on living cells, and provides a vital support role in early disease detection [2,3]. To this end, different fluorescent probes have been designed based on a broad variability of materials, such as organic dyes [4], fluorescent polymer nanoparticles [5], rare-earth-doped nanoparticles [6,7], semiconductor nanocrystals [8] and metal nanoparticles [9,10], for measuring intracellular temperature. However, those temperature probes may have at least one or more limitations, including rapid photo-bleaching, relatively tedious procedures, serious toxicity, high cost, low spatial resolution (due to the large probe size of a few hundred nanometers) and high sensitivity of their luminescent features to the specific local environment, thus restricting their potential use in biological media. By comparison, carbon dots (CDs) have received considerable attention due to their superior features such as easy preparation, relatively small size (several nanometers in diameter), excellent functionality and high photo-stability. As a result, CDs have been extensively applied in many fields, including optoelectronics, catalysis and biomedical sciences [11–14].

In view of this, substantial efforts have been made by several research groups in developing fluorescence intracellular nanothermometer based on CDs [15–22]. For example, Zbořil et al. showed that water soluble N, S-co-doped CDs exhibited temperature-dependent photoluminescence lifetimes and could serve as a highly sensitive and reliable intracellular nanothermometer [23]; Oliveira et al. performed that the CDs derived from dansyl chloride (CD-DsCl) could be used as a ratiometric thermal sensor, and the relevant contribution of a thermally-activated mechanism to the CD-DsCl fluorescence has been exhibited [24]; Kang and co-workers employed nitrogen and sulphur co-doped CDs (λem = 460 nm) as an imaging probe for detecting intracellular temperature [25]. These previous nanothermometers have many merits, but short excitation and emission wavelengths cannot be avoided, which lead to light scattering and background noise (autofluorescence of biological matrices) [26,27]. Encouragingly, the “turn-on” chemosensors with long excitation and emission wavelengths have been designed to detect temperature changes in biological media [28–31]. However, the disadvantages of weakly long-wavelength emissions or relatively lower sensitivity could not be eliminated. Therefore, it is highly imperative to develop the CD-based nanothermometers that possess strong emission at the long wavelength and exhibits fluorescence “turn-on” processes with high sensitivity.

To meet these criteria, we prepared bright-red-emissive CDs (R-CDs) and subsequently employed them as an intracellular fluorescence nanothermometer. The R-CDs with excellent fluorescence emission at 610 nm were obtained from the one-pot solvothermal synthesis of o-phenylenediamine (o-PD). The nanoprobe, as an effective temperature indicator, was almost non-fluorescent at 15 °C in aqueous solution. However, as the temperature increased from 15 °C to 60 °C, a dramatic increase in fluorescence intensity at 610 nm was observed. The fluorescence intensity values exhibited a linear response to the temperature ranging from 28 °C to 60 °C and a high-sensitive variation of ≈13.3 % °C−1 was obtained. More importantly, the proposed probe exhibited high storage stability, excellent response reversibility, superior photo- and thermo-stability. Furthermore, the proposed R-CDs were successfully applied as a novel nanothermometer for the detection of temperature changes in Hela and Salmonella cells.
o-PD was purchased from Aladdin Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All reagents used were of analytical grade without further purification. Distilled water was employed throughout the experiment.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was conducted using a GZF2.0 FEI Electron Optics (Thermo Scientific, America). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on an ESCALAB 250Xi surface analysis system (Thermo Scientific, America). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectral data were recorded on a Tensor 27 FTIR spectrometer (Bruker, Germany), using KBr pellets. Absorption measurements were performed using a Shimadzu UV-2550 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). All fluorescence spectral data were assessed using a RF5301 fluorescence spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The fluorescent lifetime and absolute quantum yields were determined by an Edinburgh Analytical FLS1000 instrument (Techcomp, China). The mixture was centrifuged using a Xiangyi H1650-W centrifuge (Xiangyi, China). Fluorescence imaging was performed with a Zeiss LSM 880 confocal laser scanning microscope (Zeiss, Germany).

The R-CD samples were prepared based on a previously described procedure with minor modifications [32]. First, a mixture solution of C2H5OH (10 mL) and HCl (60 μL) was quickly added to 0.1 g o-PD, and the resulting mixture was then placed into a 25-mL poly(tetrafluoroethylene)-lined hydrothermal autoclave. After heating for 12 h at 180 °C, the samples were cooled to room temperature, and a red suspension was formed. Following centrifugation (12,000 rpm, 15 min), a mixture of CDs was obtained, and then purified by silica column chromatography using CH2Cl2: CH3OH (30: 1, v/v) as an eluent (Fig. S1). Finally, solvent evaporation and vacuum drying were carried out, and the R-CDs were obtained.

To determine the fluorescence intensity response to the temperature variation, the fluorescence spectra were recorded at different temperatures ranging from 15 °C to 60 °C. Temperature-dependent fluorescence spectra were collected on a RF5301 fluorescence spectrometer equipped with a thermoelectrically temperature-controlled oven with a temperature control precision of ±0.1 °C. The heating and cooling of sample temperature was at a rate of 1 °C/min. The time interval between the determinations of 2 different temperatures was approximately 5 min, in order to maintain a stable internal temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro intracellular thermal sensing with R-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Before experiments, Hela cells were grown on a 6-well culture plate with coverslips at 37 °C for 24 h. The cells were then exposed to R-CDs (60 μg/mL) in RPMI medium at 37 °C for 6 h. After rinsing three times with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS, pH 7.4), temperature adjustment was carried out using an in situ heating device. Confocal fluorescence microscopy was performed using the Zeiss LSM 880 confocal laser scanning microscope. The excitation and emission wavelengths were adjusted to 543 nm and 555–655 nm, respectively. All samples were equilibrated for 5 min at the set temperature each time prior to the spectral analysis.

Salmonella bacteria were grown in LB medium at 37 °C for 24 h. After placing into a 10-mL falcon tube and centrifugation (12000 rpm, 5 min), the resulting pellet was rinsed twice with PBS (pH 7.4). The purified bacteria were plated on a confocal cell culture dish, and then exposed to R-CDs (60 μg/mL, pH 7.4) in the mixed solution of PBS: DMSO (3:2, v/v). After incubation for 12 h at 37 °C, the medium was discarded and the living cells were rinsed with distilled water. Finally, living cell imaging was performed according to the above-mentioned conditions.

Recently, o-PD was used as carbon source to prepare CDs with excellent physical and chemical properties [33–37]. Encouraged by these results, the o-PD and concentrated HCl were selected to synthesize the ideal CDs in this work. It is worthy to note that HCl plays a vital role in the generation of the high efficient red fluorescent CDs, because other acids such as H2SO4 and HNO3 may not be suitable (Fig. S2). With an increase in the volumes (10, 20, 40, 60 and 80 μL) of concentrated HCl, the yields of R-CDs were 3.3, 4.6, 6.8, 8.5 and 8.2 wt%, respectively (Fig. S3). Among the tested volumes, 60 μL of concentrated HCl was found to be the most effective (Fig. 1 A). In addition, different reaction times (3, 6, 9, 12 and 15 h) were selected for optimization, and the yields of R-CDs were 2.4, 3.1, 5.2, 8.6 and 8.4 wt%, respectively (Fig. S4). The reaction time of 12 h was considered to be the most optimal for R-CD preparation (Fig. 1B). To afford R-CDs as a black solid, the crude products were completely separated by silica column chromatography method, and the absolute quantum yield of R-CDs was measured to be 26%.

The TEM image showed that R-CDs displayed a nearly spherical shape (Fig. 2 ). Moreover, the particle-size distribution curves demonstrated that R-CDs exhibited a narrow-size distribution within the range of 2.2–5.0 nm, with a mean size of 3.6 nm, which calculated based on at least 100 particles (Fig. S5). High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (the inset of Fig. 2) indicated that the CDs had well-resolved lattice structures with an interplanar spacing of 0.21 nm, which corresponded to the in-plane lattice spacing of graphene (100 facet) [38].

The chemical state and surface composition of R-CDs were characterized by XPS and FTIR experiments. As displayed in Fig. 3 A, the XPS binding energies of R-CDs were found at 198.27, 283.21, 398.0 and 530.39 eV, which were attributed to Cl 2p3 (0.54%), C 1s (80.23%), N 1s (13.18%) and O 1s (6.05%), respectively [39]. The XPS spectrum of C 1s showed three characteristic peaks at 283.58, 286.53 and 288.90 eV (Fig. 3C), corresponding to C-C/C = C, C-N/C-O and O-C = O bonds, respectively [38]. Deconvolution of the N 1s spectrum revealed three peaks at 395.08, 398.28 and 402.53 eV (Fig. 3D), attributing to –C≡N, pyridinic N and amino N bonds, respectively [39]. The XPS spectrum of O 1s displayed only one peak at 531.98 eV (Fig. 3E), corresponding to C = O/C-OH [40]. The XPS spectrum of Cl 2p3 exhibited two peaks (Fig. 3F), assigning to benzene Cl and benzoquinone Cl [41,42]. This indicates that Cl element has been successfully incorporated into the R-CDs, and in this reaction, concentrated HCl may not only react as carbonization agent, but also actually react as the reactant. Moreover, FTIR analysis was performed to examine the covalent bonds on R-CD surface (Fig. 3B). The FTIR spectrum of R-CDs showed numerous characteristic peaks, such as stretching vibration of C-O-C at 1122 cm−1, bending vibration of C-O at 1325 cm−1, skeletal vibration of aromatic ring (νC=C) at 1452 and 1576 cm−1, stretching vibration of O = C-O- at 1726 cm−1, stretching modes of C-H at 2925 cm−1 and stretching vibration of –NH2 at 3228 and 3349 cm−1. Collectively, both XPS and FTIR findings indicated that the surface of R-CDs has been successfully functionalized with carboxylic and amino acid groups, which act as a passivation layer and endow them a high solubility and strong fluorescent emission.

As shown in Fig. 4 A, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of R-CDs exhibited one characteristic peak at 282 nm, corresponding to the π to π* transition of C = C (or C = N) bonds [43], but no fluorescent signal was observed. Meanwhile, a broad absorption band was detected at 534 nm with a shoulder at 564 nm, attributing to the n to π* transition from the molecule surfaces, thereby generating a strong fluorescent signal [44]. The fluorescence excitation spectrum displayed a prominent peak at 536 and 566 nm, which was relatively near to its absorption peak in the low-energy region. This suggests that the optical absorption and luminescence spectra of R-CDs are identical [38]. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity increased first and subsequently decreased over an excitation wavelength range of 450–590 nm (Fig. 4B). The maximal excitation wavelength of R-CDs was 540 nm, and it exhibited dual fluorescence bands at 602 and 644 nm in ethanol solution, attributing to core and surface state emission, respectively [45]. It is worth noting that the maximum fluorescence intensity does not evidently shift with increasing excitation wavelengths. This excitation-independent performance of R-CDs may be due to the good chemical composition and content uniformity of R-CDs [46], which can avoid the phenomenon of auto-fluorescence during live cell imaging [47].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Insight into the temperature-dependent fluorescence of R-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The influence of concentration of R-CDs on their fluorescent intensity at 610 nm was firstly investigated when the temperature was 60 °C (Fig. S6). As the concentration of the R-CD solution increased from 1 to 40 μg/mL, the fluorescent intensity at 610 nm initially increased before reaching its maximal value at 20 μg/mL. Thus, the concentration of the R-CD solution was chosen to be 20 μg/mL. Subsequently, to investigate the potential application of R-CDs, the fluorescence spectra within a wide temperature range of 15–60 °C were analyzed. As the temperature increased, the fluorescence intensity at 610 nm exhibited a gradual increase and the final enhancement factor was greater than 21-fold (Fig. 5 A). Moreover, a linear relationship was found between fluorescence intensity and temperature, according to the linear regression equation F 610nm = 13.58 T – 332.82, with a linear coefficient of 0.9983 (Fig. 5B). The sensitive variation of fluorescence intensity was determined to be ≈13.3 % °C−1 within a broad temperature range of 28–60 °C, which was comparable or even better than that of the existing fluorescence nanothermometers [48,49]. In addition, a distinguishable color change of the R-CDs was detected at 20 °C and 60 °C (Inset of Fig. 5A).

According to the previous literature [50], such an enhancement in the emission intensity indicates that a thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF) may take place in the R-CDs. To elucidate the mechanism underlying the responses to temperature, time-resolved fluorescence measurements were firstly conducted in different time scales (nanosecond and microsecond). As shown in Fig. 6 A, the R-CDs exhibited bi-exponential decays on the nanosecond time and microsecond time scale, and the average lifetime were 2.65 ns and 5.11 μs, respectively, corresponding to the delayed fluorescence. Moreover, the energy gap ΔE ST was estimated to investigate the mechanism [24]:
(1)
l n
∅
T
∅
T 0
=
Δ E
ST
k B
1
T 0
-
1 T
where ΔE ST is the energy gap between the lowest singlet state (S 1) and the lowest triplet state (T 1), while k B is the Boltzmann constant and T 0 is a reference temperature. As shown in Fig. 6B, the quantum yields increased as the sample temperature was raised. By using Eq. (1), the energy gap ΔE ST could be estimate from the angular or linear coefficients of the linear regression, with ΔE ST = 54.4 meV (<100 meV). Thus, the triplet excitons could effectively transfer back from the T 1 to S 1 excited states as the sample temperature is raised, and a radiative transition occurs from S1 to ground state (S0), resulting in the enhancement of the emission intensity [51]. Therefore, we speculated that the enhanced fluorescent intensity may be attributed to the TADF mechanism.

Subsquently, TEM measurement was also performed to explain the temperature-induced fluorescence enhancing effect. The average diameter of R-CDs at 45 °C was 1.8 nm, and the R-CDs showed good mono-dispersion (Fig. S7). Thus, a decrease in the diameter of CDs between 25 °C and 45 °C was noted. Furthermore, the as-prepared CDs displayed an obvious change in the UV–vis spectra (Fig. S8). When the temperature increased from 15 °C to 60 °C, the absorbance of R-CD solution at 534 nm elevated gradually without obvious absorption peak shift, which was consistent with the fluorescence spectra. Therefore, we concluded that, with increasing temperature, the evident fluorescence enhancement may not only be attributed to the TADF mechanism, but also partly due to the dissociation of R-CD aggregates.

The stability of CDs is a crucial factor for evaluating their potential applications. Hence, the effects of storage time, irradiation time, ionic strength, and ions-stability on the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs were determined. Ions-stability was assessed by interfering the as-prepared fluorescent CDs with different concentrations of KCl solutions and some common ions. The results revealed that these ions had no interference, indicating that high ionic strength and these common ions may not play a part in affecting the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs (Fig. S9 and S10). Then, the photostability of R-CDs at different temperature was tested by measuring the fluorescent response under daylight and UV light for 2 h. Notably, the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs at 610 nm exhibited no obvious changes even after 2 h, indicating that this nanoprobe performed good stability upon daylight and UV light irradiation (Fig. S11). In addition, the R-CDs in aqueous solution could be stored at room temperature for one month (Fig. S12). Based on these findings, it can be inferred that R-CDs possess high ions- and photo-stability as well as long-term storage. These characteristics make them a suitable candidate for applications as bio-sensor or imaging device.

Besides, the repeatability of the CD-based fluorescent nanothermometer was also evaluated. The results showed that the thermal responses of R-CDs were reversible for ten continuous heating–cooling cycles between 30 °C and 60 °C (Fig. S13), suggesting that this novel nanothermometer has good reusability. Due to these advantageous characteristics, (e.g., high sensitivity, broad response range, good stability and repeatability, and linear temperature-dependent emission), our proposed R-CDs can be utilized as a “turn-on” fluorescent nanothermometer.

Subsequently, the changes in the fluorescence intensity values of Salmonella cells were evaluated after exposure to R-CDs under various temperature conditions (Fig. 9 ). It was found that the stained Salmonella cells displayed slight red fluorescence emission at 30 °C. When the extracellular temperature was increased to 40 °C, the red fluorescence intensity increased sharply. These results are consistent with those of Hela cells, thus confirming that R-CDs could serve as a fluorescence-based nanothermometer for monitoring intracellular temperature.

Lastly, the characteristics of our proposed R-CDs were compared with the existing fluorescence methods for temperature sensing. As presented in Table 1 , previous nanothermometers have many advantages (e.g., high accuracy, wide response range), but drawbacks cannot be excluded (e.g., blue light emission, tedious synthesis procedures, large particle size or low sensitivity) [17,19,28,48,52–55]. On the contrary, our nanothermometer, performing relatively smaller particle size and longer-wavelength emission, exhibits high sensitivity, chemical- and photo-stability, and simple synthesis, separation and purification steps, which is conducive to living cell imaging. The major limitation of the nanothermometer is the single emission-based fluorescent assay, which still needs to be improved for accurate detection of intracellular temperature values [56].

In this study, we constructed a novel “turn-on” fluorescent nanothermometer based on CDs with long-wavelength emission. As the temperature increased, the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs exhibited a gradual increase, and the enhancement factor was more than 21-fold with a high sensitivity of ≈13.3 % °C−1. This indicates that R-CDs have a high accuracy and sensitivity for detecting temperature changes. Moreover, this novel nanothermometer was demonstrated to have good biocompatibility, high photo- and thermal-stability, and excellent accuracy for detecting temperature changes in living cells. The fluorescent “turn-on” probes described herein could reduce false-positive signals and the dark background, which have great potential for monitoring temperature in biological matrices.

Qin Wang: Investigation, Writing – original draft. Zhihua Tang: . Lihua Li: . Jinxiu Guo: . Lingxia Jin: . Jiufu Lu: . Pei Huang: . Shengrui Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Long Jiao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21775118, 22177066), Innovation Capability Support Program of Shaanxi (Nos. 2020KJXX-030, 2021KJXX-51), Scientific Research Program Funded by Education Department of Shaanxi Provincial Government (20JK0575), Co-construction Project of Hanzhong city and Shaanxi University of Technology (SXJ-2103, SXJ-2105) and Open Foundation of Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Natural Functional Molecule Chemistry of Ministry of Education (KLSNFM2020007).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2022.121538.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Luminescent probes and sensors for temperature

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X.D. Wang, O.S. Wolfbeis, R.J. Meier, Luminescent probes and sensors for temperature, Chem. Soc. Rev., 42 (2013) 7834-7869.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nanoscale temperature mapping in operating microelectronic devices

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Mecklenburg, W.A. Hubbard, E. White, R. Dhall, S.B. Cronin, S. Aloni, B. Regan, Nanoscale temperature mapping in operating microelectronic devices, Science, 347 (2015) 629-632.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nanometre-scale thermometry in a living cell

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Kucsko, P.C. Maurer, N.Y. Yao, M. Kubo, H.J. Noh, P.K. Lo, H. Park, M.D. Lukin, Nanometre-scale thermometry in a living cell, Nature, 500 (2013) 54-58.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: TICT-based near-Infrared ratiometric organic fluorescent thermometer for intracellular temperature sensing

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L.C. Meng, S. Jiang, M.Y. Song, F. Yan, W. Zhang, B. Xu, W.J. Tian, TICT-based near-Infrared ratiometric organic fluorescent thermometer for intracellular temperature sensing, ACS Appl. Mater. Inter., 12 (2020) 26842-26851.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simultaneous monitoring of temperature and Ca2+ concentration variation by fluorescent polymer during intracellular heat production

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Qiao, Y.H. Hwang, D.P. Kim, L. Qi, Simultaneous monitoring of temperature and Ca2+ concentration variation by fluorescent polymer during intracellular heat production, Anal. Chem., 92 (2020) 8579-8583.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Reliability of rare-earth-doped infrared luminescent nanothermometers

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Labrador-Paez, M. Pedroni, A. Speghini, J. Garcia-Sole, P. Haro-Gonzalez, D. Jaque, Reliability of rare-earth-doped infrared luminescent nanothermometers, Nanoscale, 10 (2018) 22319-22328.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Temperature sensing of deep abdominal region in mice by using over-1000 nm near-infrared luminescence of rare-earth-doped NaYF4 nanothermometer

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Sekiyama, M. Umezawa, S. Kuraoka, T. Ube, M. Kamimura, K. Soga, Temperature sensing of deep abdominal region in mice by using over-1000 nm near-infrared luminescence of rare-earth-doped NaYF4 nanothermometer, Sci. Rep., 8 (2018) 16979.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Conducting the temperature-dependent conformational change of macrocyclic compounds to the lattice dilation of quantum dots for achieving an ultrasensitive nanothermometer
